meta.region: Cortes Island and immediately adjacent waters/territories, British Columbia
meta.note: Esoterica: royalty, unsolved murder, sasquatch lore, deep-time geology, oral tradition. 'confidence' flags: documented / reported / oral_tradition / legend. Coordinates approximate. Items marked 'regional' sit just off Cortes but belong to its story. Generated by Claude, curated 2026-07-05.

items.name: Tho'kwet — 'Tied Together'
items.description: Twin Islands' Tla'amin name, describing the two islands joined by a drying spit. A seasonal food-gathering site long before any lodge.

items.name: The Unsolved Murder of Rev. Harpur Nixon
items.description: Twin Islands first made news during WWI when owner Reverend Harpur Nixon was shot. The killing was never solved — the coast's quietest cold case, sitting in plain sight of Hollyhock.

items.name: A Lodge Built by War Prophecy
items.description: Americans Dick and Ethel Andrews predicted WWII, pulled their capital out of their Japan import business in 1936, and hired Scandinavian immigrants from Lund to build the massive log-and-stone Twin Islands lodge on-site (completed 1938).

items.name: Disney and Crosby at Twin
items.description: Under the 1956 ownership group (Vancouver Sun editor Hal Straight and two Alberta oilmen), guests including Walt Disney and Bing Crosby visited Twin Islands — a sampler for an exclusive resort that never materialized.

items.name: Cold War Royal Bolthole
items.description: German royalty — Maximilian, Margrave of Baden, nephew of Prince Philip — bought Twin Islands in the early 1960s as a remote getaway and offshore asset at the height of the Cold War.

items.name: The Queen's Picnic, 1971
items.description: Queen Elizabeth II and family picnicked on Twin Islands during a 1971 cruise — first of two royal visits to the islands off Cortes's southeast shore.

items.name: The Secret Royal Stay, 1994
items.description: After opening the Victoria Commonwealth Games in August 1994, the Queen and Prince Philip vanished from public view — to Twin Islands, arriving by seaplane. Cortes knew: islander Ginny Ellingsen had been hired to wait on them.

items.name: Serenading the Monarch
items.description: During the 1994 visit, Cortes naturalist George Sirk arranged for his classically trained wife, singer Kim Paulley, to perform for the royal party — a Cortes-flavoured ambush of hospitality.

items.name: The Heiress Who Bought the Logging Show
items.description: When 1990s owners began logging Twin Islands (~33,000 m³ cut on the south island), Carol Newell — an heiress involved with Hollyhock, which overlooks Twin — told her advisors to find a way to stop it 'even if it means buying the islands.' A deal was struck within a year.

items.name: Restoration Era at Twin
items.description: Mark Torrance and Susan Summers, shopping for 'a few acres with a cabin' in 1998, were shown Twin Islands instead. They restored the 1938 lodge and gardens and added renewable energy systems; a commissioned history by Jeanette Taylor documents the whole saga.

items.name: The Ostman Abduction
items.description: Prospector Albert Ostman claimed a sasquatch carried him off in his sleeping bag near the head of Toba Inlet — Klahoose heartland northeast of Cortes — in 1924 and held him six days with its family. He escaped, he said, by feeding the patriarch his snuff tin.

items.name: Sworn Before a Magistrate
items.description: Ostman sat on the story for 33 years, then told it in 1957, signed a Solemn Declaration under the Canada Evidence Act, and was cross-examined by magistrate A.M. Naismith, who wrote he could find no flaw 'in either his personality or his story.' Skeptics note the delay and the implausible vegan menu.

items.name: The Lost Gold Mine of Toba
items.description: The frame story of Ostman's trip: a lost mine at the head of Toba Inlet, worked by a hard-drinking white man who periodically returned to the saloons with bags of gold — until one trip he never came back. 'Some people said a Sasquatch had killed him.'

items.name: 1924: The Sasquatch Year
items.description: Ostman's Toba encounter and Washington State's Ape Canyon incident (miners besieged by rock-throwing 'ape-men') both date to 1924 — the founding year of modern sasquatch lore, bracketing the Salish Sea.

items.name: 'They Are People'
items.description: Ostman's guide from Lund described sasquatch in explicitly non-animal terms: 'They have hair all over their bodies, but they are not animals. They are people. Big people living in the mountains.' This matches how wild-people figures function in Coast Salish oral tradition — persons, not beasts.

items.name: Two Kilometres of Ice
items.description: At the last glacial maximum the Cordilleran Ice Sheet buried Cortes under roughly 2 km of ice. Every lake basin, gorge, and rounded bluff on the island is glacial furniture.

items.name: Shorelines 197 Metres Up
items.description: As ice retreated ~14,300 years ago, the crust was so depressed that relative sea level in the Quadra–Cortes area stood at least 197 m above today's. The oldest local beaches are high in the forest.

items.name: The Fastest Sea-Level Fall
items.description: Rebounding land dropped relative sea level from ~197 m to within 2–3 m of modern in about two thousand years (by ~12,000 years ago) — one of the fastest shoreline migrations humans anywhere have lived through. Raised marine terraces at ~4, 10 and 30 m record pauses.

items.name: Ice-Age Sites Above Your Head
items.description: Because local sea level fell rather than rose, the region's earliest human sites sit preserved on raised shorelines in the forest instead of drowned offshore — the inverse of Puget Sound. Hakai-affiliated teams model paleoshorelines around Quadra to hunt for them.

items.name: 13,000-Year-Old Cockle
items.description: A cockle shell from marine sediment 50 m above present sea level on central Quadra dated to ~13,500–13,100 years ago — shellfish beds from the end of the ice age, now mid-island.

items.name: Oldest Clam Garden on Record
items.description: A clam garden wall in the Kanish/Waiatt Bay complex on northern Quadra dates to ~3,500 years ago — the oldest documented anywhere, pushing known mariculture back ~2,000 years. Cortes beaches carry the same technology; the walls were identified to Western science only in the mid-1990s by geomorphologist John Harper.

items.name: Engineering in Three Forms
items.description: Researchers identify three distinct clam-garden engineering forms adapted to different beach geologies — walls on soft sediment, bedrock, and boulder beaches. Not piles of rocks: a differentiated technology, tuned per site, sustaining higher populations than the coast holds today.

items.name: The Flood Narratives
items.description: Coast Salish oral traditions across the region describe a great flood survived by tying canoes to mountain peaks. Read against the region's documented extreme post-glacial sea-level swings, these narratives are increasingly treated as long-memory accounts rather than pure myth.

items.name: Church House / ʔəms tečƛ̓ (regional)
items.description: The abandoned Homalco village at the entrance to Bute Inlet, its church visible from the water, was largely vacated by the 1970s–80s as residents moved to Campbell River. A haunting landmark on the route north from Cortes; Homalco-led revitalization efforts continue.

items.name: June Cameron's Cortez
items.description: Sailor-writer June Cameron, whose grandparents arrived in 1917, recorded pioneer Cortes in 'Destination Cortez Island' — including the failed orchard economics: no reliable freight for perishables sank family after family. She kept the 'Cortez'/'Cor-teez' usage her whole life.

items.name: Jeanette Taylor's 'Tidal Passages'
items.description: The definitive regional history covering Cortes ('you couldn't make this stuff up' is Taylor's own line about Twin Islands). Her Twin Islands book exists only as a limited private publication sold at the Museum at Campbell River — a genuinely rare document.

items.name: Rex Weyler's Vigil
items.description: Greenpeace co-founder Rex Weyler has lived on Cortes for decades and now teaches natural history with island students through the Wild Cortes partnership — the ecological memory of the anti-whaling movement residing on an island named by whalers' absence.

items.name: Ruth Ozeki's Time Being
items.description: Novelist and Zen priest Ruth Ozeki wrote 'A Tale for the Time Being' (2013) from Whaletown; the narrator 'Ruth' lives on a remote BC island and finds a Japanese diary washed ashore in a Hello Kitty lunchbox — Cortes rendered as autofiction, complete with tsunami-debris beachcombing.

items.name: Arctic Terns, 3,000 Miles Off Course
items.description: A Misty Isles Adventures skipper's 'secret location' near Twin Islands has produced Arctic tern sightings estimated at 3,000 miles from expected range — the kind of anomaly a small island's naturalists quietly accumulate.

items.name: Carol Newell's Quiet Empire
items.description: The heiress who saved Twin Islands seeded much of the region's conservation-finance ecosystem (Endswell Foundation, Renewal Partners), largely anonymously for years — Cortes and Hollyhock sat at the centre of one of Canada's least-publicized philanthropic experiments.

items.name: The Guide from Lund
items.description: The unnamed Tla'amin-area elder who boated Ostman up Toba in 1924 is the story's real artifact: his matter-of-fact sasquatch ethnography and lost-mine account preserve a snapshot of local oral tradition that predates all modern bigfoot culture by decades.
